lectures.title: Endo1_2: Overview of the Endocrine System & Neuroendocrine Systems

questions.q: Which scientist first defined the word 'hormone' in 1905?
questions.explain: Ernest Starling coined 'hormone' from the Greek 'to arouse or excite' in 1905. He and Bayliss discovered secretin, the first hormone, in 1902.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a classical endocrine gland but rather an endocrine cell in another tissue?
questions.explain: G cells in the stomach are endocrine cells within the stomach wall, not a classical endocrine gland. They secrete gastrin. The thyroid, adrenal, pineal, and pancreatic islets are classical endocrine glands.

questions.q: Which of the following cannot serve as a hormone in the body?
questions.explain: Hormones are derived from amino acids, peptides, steroids (cholesterol), or prostaglandins (arachidonic acid). Glucose derivatives are not used as hormones.

questions.q: Prostaglandins are lipid-soluble but differ from other lipid-soluble hormones because they:
options: Bind to surface receptors coupled with cAMP signaling
Are secreted by the pituitary gland
questions.explain: Prostaglandins are lipid-soluble yet bind surface receptors coupled with cAMP signaling, unlike other lipid-soluble hormones which use nuclear receptors. This is a key exam distinction.

questions.q: Lipid-soluble hormones exert their effects primarily by:
options: Acting on DNA to modify transcription
questions.explain: Lipid-soluble hormones (steroids and thyroxine) bind cytoplasmic or nuclear receptors; the hormone-receptor complex then acts directly on DNA to modify transcription. Effects last hours to days.

questions.q: Which hormone type is produced from arachidonic acid?
questions.explain: Prostaglandins (and related eicosanoids) are synthesized from arachidonic acid, a 20-carbon polyunsaturated fatty acid derived from membrane phospholipids.

questions.q: NSAIDs such as aspirin reduce fever and pain by:
questions.explain: NSAIDs inhibit the enzymes that form prostaglandins involved in fever, pain, and inflammation. This is why they are effective anti-inflammatory and analgesic drugs.

questions.q: Insulin uses which type of receptor?
questions.explain: Insulin binds receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) which have intrinsic kinase activity and autophosphorylate upon ligand binding. This is distinct from GPCR-coupled hormone signaling.

questions.q: Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) receptor is a:
questions.explain: ANP binds a receptor guanylate cyclase; ligand binding triggers formation of cGMP as the second messenger. This is different from most peptide hormones that use cAMP or IP3/DAG.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of insulin?
options: It is first synthesized as a larger precursor
It utilizes cell membrane receptors coupled with G protein
questions.explain: Insulin uses receptor tyrosine kinases, NOT G protein-coupled receptors. It is water soluble, produced from a precursor, and consists of two polypeptide chains (A and B chains).

questions.q: Neurosecretory cells in the hypothalamus differ from ordinary neurons structurally in that they have:
options: Larger secretory granules (100-400 nm) versus synaptic vesicles (30-60 nm)
Direct synaptic connections to target organs
questions.explain: Neurosecretory cells have secretory granules of 100-400 nm, much larger than ordinary synaptic vesicles (30-60 nm). They terminate in capillary beds (neurohemal organs) rather than on specific post-synaptic cells.

questions.q: Which statement about the hypothalamus is TRUE?
options: It is located above the thalamus
It receives hormones from neurosecretory cells
It secretes hormones into the hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system
It is the part of brain primarily involved in memory
It is part of the adenohypophysis
questions.explain: The hypothalamus secretes releasing and inhibiting hormones from parvocellular neurons into the hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system, which then delivers them to the anterior pituitary.

questions.q: The anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis) is:
options: Neural tissue consisting of axons from the hypothalamus
True endocrine tissue secreting regular hormones
The source of ADH and oxytocin
Connected to the posterior pituitary by the portal system
questions.explain: The anterior pituitary is true endocrine tissue (adenohypophysis = gland underneath). It is connected to the hypothalamus via the portal system, not nerve axons. The posterior pituitary is neural tissue.

questions.q: Which of the following is a tropic hormone from the anterior pituitary?
questions.explain: FSH is a tropic hormone that acts on other endocrine tissues (the gonads). GH, prolactin, and MSH are direct-acting hormones. Oxytocin comes from the posterior pituitary.

questions.q: Prolactin secretion is stimulated by which hypothalamic factor?
questions.explain: Prolactin is stimulated by TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) and estrogen, and inhibited by dopamine (which acts as prolactin-inhibiting hormone). This is clinically important in hyperprolactinemia.

questions.q: MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) is generated by cleavage of which precursor?
questions.explain: MSH is generated by different cleavages of proopiomelanocortin (POMC), which also yields ACTH. High CRH (as in Addison's disease) elevates both ACTH and MSH, causing skin darkening.

questions.q: Melasma (dark skin patches) during pregnancy is caused by elevated:
questions.explain: Pregnancy raises estrogen and progesterone, which stimulate MSH secretion. MSH acts on melanocytes to produce more melanin, causing melasma. Sun exposure worsens it.

questions.q: Which direct-acting hormone from the anterior pituitary has a release-inhibiting hormone from the hypothalamus?
questions.explain: Only direct-acting hormones (GH, prolactin) have both releasing and release-inhibiting hormones. GH has GRH and somatostatin (GIH). Tropic hormones like FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH generally only have releasing hormones.

questions.q: Magnocellular neurons in the SON and PVN project to the:
options: Anterior pituitary via the portal system
questions.explain: Magnocellular neurons in the supraoptic nucleus (SON) and paraventricular nucleus (PVN) project their axons to the posterior pituitary, where ADH and oxytocin are released into the bloodstream.

questions.q: ADH (vasopressin) promotes water reabsorption in the kidney by:
questions.explain: ADH receptors are coupled with adenylate cyclase; the resulting cAMP increase causes insertion of aquaporin water channels into the apical membrane of kidney collecting duct cells, increasing water permeability.

questions.q: Oxytocin targets which two organs?
questions.explain: Oxytocin stimulates milk ejection from the mammary gland during nursing and uterine contractions during and after childbirth. It also acts as a neurotransmitter in the brain, involved in bonding.

questions.q: Which statement about prolactin and oxytocin is TRUE?
options: Both share the same target tissue
Both are secreted by the anterior pituitary
questions.explain: Both prolactin (from anterior pituitary) and oxytocin (from posterior pituitary) stimulate aspects of milk production/ejection. Prolactin stimulates milk protein synthesis; oxytocin triggers milk ejection. They are not both neurohormones, nor from the same gland.

questions.q: Which of the following is a neurohormone?
questions.explain: ADH (vasopressin) is a neurohormone synthesized in neurosecretory cells of the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary. GH, insulin, prolactin, and LH are regular hormones from glandular cells.

questions.q: The hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system consists of:
options: A single capillary bed draining into the systemic circulation
Two capillary beds directly joined by blood vessels
A lymphatic network connecting hypothalamus to pituitary
Direct nerve innervation of the anterior pituitary
Axons of parvocellular neurons synapsing in the anterior pituitary
questions.explain: The portal system has two capillary beds: one in the hypothalamus where parvocellular neurons release hormones, and one in the anterior pituitary where those hormones act. They are directly joined by portal veins.

questions.q: Which pair of hormones is NOT closely related (i.e., does not have a correct releasing hormone pairing)?
questions.explain: GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone) controls FSH and LH, not growth hormone. GH is controlled by GRH and somatostatin (GIH). This is a common exam trap.

questions.q: The endocrine system differs from the nervous system in that it:
options: Acts over longer time scales (minutes to days)
Cannot be influenced by neural stimuli
questions.explain: The endocrine system acts over longer distances via the bloodstream and sustains regulatory functions for minutes to days. The nervous system acts rapidly (milliseconds). Both systems interact closely.

questions.q: Which is TRUE about hormone secretion regulation?
options: Only humoral changes regulate hormone secretion
Neural stimuli cannot regulate hormone secretion
Hormonal stimuli, humoral changes, and neural stimuli all regulate secretion
Blood pressure never affects hormone secretion
questions.explain: Hormone secretion is regulated by three mechanisms: (1) humoral changes (e.g., blood Ca++, glucose, osmolarity), (2) neural stimuli (e.g., sympathetic control of adrenal medulla), and (3) hormonal stimuli (e.g., hypothalamic neurohormones controlling pituitary).

questions.q: Thyroxine (T4) is derived from which amino acid?
questions.explain: Thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) and catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine) are all derived from tyrosine. Melatonin is derived from tryptophan.

questions.q: Which class of hormones requires ethanol (not water) as a solvent for stock solution preparation?
questions.explain: Steroid hormones (like cortisol) are lipid-soluble and do not dissolve in water or saline. Ethanol is used to dissolve steroids for stock solutions in laboratory experiments. Peptides dissolve in water.

questions.q: Which of the following is a stimulus for GH secretion?
questions.explain: Deep sleep stimulates GH secretion, with a peak about 1 hour after sleep onset. High blood glucose, REM sleep, and somatostatin inhibit GH secretion. Arginine and stress also stimulate GH.

lectures.title: Endo3_4: Metabolic & Developmental Hormones + Water/Electrolyte Balance

questions.q: The adrenal medulla is embryologically derived from:
options: Neural (ectodermal) tissue, like sympathetic postganglionic neurons
Neural crest only, distinct from sympathetic neurons
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla is neural (ectodermal) in origin, analogous to sympathetic postganglionic neurons. The adrenal cortex is mesodermal. Chromaffin cells are the modified postganglionic neurons of the adrenal medulla.

questions.q: Which statement about the adrenal medulla is FALSE?
options: It is the inner layer of the adrenal gland
It is considered an extension of the sympathetic nervous system
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla secretes catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) only. Glucocorticoids are secreted by the adrenal CORTEX (outer layer), not the medulla.

questions.q: Catecholamine secretion from the adrenal medulla is regulated by:
options: None of the above — it is regulated by the sympathetic nervous system
questions.explain: The adrenal medulla is regulated by preganglionic sympathetic neurons. It is NOT regulated by ACTH, GH, or insulin. There is no hormone called 'chromaffinotropic hormone.'

questions.q: Which is NOT an effect of epinephrine?
questions.explain: Epinephrine INHIBITS insulin secretion (via alpha-2 adrenoreceptors) and INCREASES glucagon secretion, raising blood glucose. It also increases glycogenolysis, lipolysis, and causes bronchodilation.

questions.q: Glucocorticoids primarily act to promote gluconeogenesis in which organ?
questions.explain: Glucocorticoids primarily stimulate gluconeogenesis in the liver (formation of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors). They also inhibit glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue, and promote protein degradation and lipolysis.

questions.q: Cortisol release is stimulated by which sequence?
questions.explain: Stress triggers CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) from hypothalamic parvocellular neurons, which stimulates ACTH from the anterior pituitary, which in turn stimulates cortisol from the adrenal cortex. Negative feedback by cortisol acts on both hypothalamus and pituitary.

questions.q: Cushing's syndrome results from:
options: Excess cortisol from drugs, tumors producing cortisol, ACTH, or CRH
Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex
questions.explain: Cushing's syndrome is caused by excessive cortisol, from glucocorticoid drugs, cortisol-secreting tumors, or ACTH/CRH-secreting tumors. Addison's disease (not Cushing's) results from cortisol deficiency.

questions.q: Which is a correct statement about cortisol?
options: Cortisol = hydrocortisone, and it is the major glucocorticoid in humans
Cortisol is secreted by the adrenal medulla
Cortisol has no effect on the immune system
Cortisol promotes glucose uptake in muscle
questions.explain: Cortisol is also known as hydrocortisone and is the major glucocorticoid in humans. It is secreted by the adrenal CORTEX, not the medulla. It suppresses the immune system and INHIBITS (not promotes) glucose uptake in muscle.

questions.q: Thyroid hormones T3 and T4 are synthesized from:
questions.explain: T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine/tetraiodothyronine) are formed from two iodinated tyrosine precursors in the thyroid. Dietary iodine deficiency causes goiter because the thyroid enlarges trying to compensate.

questions.q: For people with hyperthyroidism, the increased heart rate and strength is related to thyroid hormones:
options: Stimulating catecholamine secretion from the adrenal medulla
Stimulating cellular respiration in the heart
questions.explain: Thyroid hormones sensitize tissues to epinephrine (catecholamines). In hyperthyroidism, elevated T3/T4 increase beta-adrenergic receptor density, amplifying catecholamine effects including increased heart rate.

questions.q: In target tissues, which thyroid hormone form is more potent?
options: T4 is more potent than T3
T3 is more potent; T4 is converted to T3 in target tissues
rT3 (reverse T3) is the active form
questions.explain: T4 (thyroxine) is the major hormone released from the thyroid, but in target tissues it is converted to the more potent T3 (triiodothyronine). T3 binds more avidly to nuclear receptors and has greater biological activity.

questions.q: Insulin secretion is INHIBITED by:
options: Activation of the sympathetic nervous system
Activation of the parasympathetic nervous system
questions.explain: Sympathetic nervous system activation inhibits insulin release (via alpha-2 adrenoreceptors), which is part of the fight-or-flight response. Parasympathetic activation, elevated glucose, amino acids, and GIP all stimulate insulin.

questions.q: Which statement about insulin and glucagon is FALSE?
options: Both secretions are stimulated by elevated amino acid levels
Both secretions are regulated by blood glucose level
Both are secreted by alpha cells in the islets of Langerhans
Glucagon promotes conversion of amino acids to glucose
questions.explain: Insulin is secreted by BETA cells, and glucagon by ALPHA cells in the islets of Langerhans. Both secretions are stimulated by elevated amino acids, and both are regulated by blood glucose (but in opposite directions).

questions.q: A protein-rich, carbohydrate-low meal stimulates both insulin and glucagon. The beneficial effect is:
options: Preventing hypoglycemia while also promoting protein anabolism
questions.explain: Insulin promotes amino acid uptake and protein synthesis in muscle/liver; glucagon promotes conversion of amino acids to glucose. Together they prevent hypoglycemia (glucagon) while driving protein anabolism (insulin) after a protein-rich meal.

questions.q: In type 1 diabetes, ketoacidosis occurs because:
options: Glucagon is absent, so no ketone formation
Absence of insulin means glucagon is not inhibited, leading to excess glucagon and increased ketone formation by the liver
High glucose directly causes ketone production
The kidneys fail to filter ketones
questions.explain: In type 1 diabetes, absence of insulin removes the inhibitory effect of insulin on glucagon secretion. High glucagon levels drive ketone formation in the liver. Excess ketones cause ketoacidosis, leading to electrolyte imbalance and potential coma.

questions.q: Which is NOT usually associated with type 1 diabetes?
questions.explain: In type 1 diabetes, glucagon levels are HIGH (not low) because insulin normally suppresses glucagon, and that inhibition is absent. All other options are associated with type 1 DM.

questions.q: Growth hormone stimulates insulin secretion by:
options: Direct action on beta cells and indirect hyperglycemic effect
questions.explain: GH stimulates insulin in two ways: (1) direct action on pancreatic beta cells, and (2) indirectly through its hyperglycemic effects which then stimulate insulin secretion.

questions.q: Most of the growth-promoting effects of GH are mediated by:
options: IGF-1 and IGF-2 (somatomedins) released mainly from the liver
questions.explain: GH stimulates the liver to produce IGF-1 (and some IGF-2), which mediate most of the linear growth and tissue-building effects of GH. GH also has some direct effects, particularly its anti-insulin metabolic actions.

questions.q: Which correctly describes the metabolic effects of GH?
options: Promotes glucose uptake in all tissues
questions.explain: GH has paradoxical metabolic effects: insulin-like effects (promotes amino acid uptake, protein synthesis in muscle) AND anti-insulin effects (decreases glucose uptake, increases lipolysis, promotes gluconeogenesis). The net effect tends toward hyperglycemia.

questions.q: GH deficiency during childhood leads to:
questions.explain: GH deficiency in childhood leads to dwarfism (failure of linear bone growth). Excess GH in childhood causes gigantism; excess in adulthood causes acromegaly (enlarged jaw, nose, hands). The Laron dwarfs have GH receptor mutations.

questions.q: Leptin is encoded by the obese (ob) gene and acts primarily to:
options: Increase appetite and decrease energy expenditure
Decrease appetite and increase energy expenditure
questions.explain: Leptin (from Greek leptos = thin) reduces appetite and increases energy expenditure. High leptin signals adequate fat stores. Leptin receptor (LepR) is primarily expressed in the brain; most effects are mediated via the CNS.

questions.q: ADH secretion is stimulated by a decrease in blood volume of:
questions.explain: Osmotic regulation of ADH is very sensitive (5-10 mOsmol change). Volume regulation requires a larger stimulus: greater than 10% decrease in blood volume, as after hemorrhage, triggers ADH secretion via stretch receptors in the cardiovascular system.

questions.q: Alcohol causes increased urination by:
options: Acting as a diuretic on the kidney directly
Activating stretch receptors in the atria
questions.explain: Alcohol potently inhibits ADH release from the posterior pituitary. Without ADH, the kidney cannot reabsorb water, leading to increased urine volume and subsequent thirst and dehydration.

questions.q: Which will NOT stimulate ADH secretion?
options: Drinking a large volume of water
questions.explain: Drinking a large volume of water decreases plasma osmolality, which REDUCES ADH secretion (allowing more water excretion to restore normal osmolality). All other options increase osmolality or decrease blood volume/pressure, stimulating ADH.

questions.q: The primary factor regulating aldosterone secretion is:
questions.explain: Angiotensin II is the primary hormonal regulator of aldosterone secretion. It is generated when blood pressure falls, triggering the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Extracellular K+ and ACTH also regulate aldosterone but are secondary factors.

questions.q: Aldosterone action on the kidney results in:
options: Na+ reabsorption, K+ excretion, and increased extracellular fluid volume
questions.explain: Aldosterone stimulates Na+ reabsorption and K+ excretion in the collecting duct. Water follows sodium, increasing extracellular fluid volume and blood pressure. This is the opposite of ANP effects.

questions.q: Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) is released in response to:
options: Increased blood volume causing atrial stretch
questions.explain: ANP is secreted by endocrine cells in the cardiac atria in response to mechanical stretch from increased blood volume. It counteracts aldosterone and ADH by promoting Na+ excretion and urine production, lowering blood pressure.

questions.q: Which correctly describes ADH, aldosterone, and ANP?
options: All work on the same target tissue
ANP opposes ADH and aldosterone by promoting Na+ excretion
questions.explain: ANP opposes both ADH (which retains water) and aldosterone (which retains Na+) by promoting Na+ and water excretion. ADH acts on kidney and blood vessels; aldosterone acts on kidney; ANP also acts on kidney. They do not all increase blood pressure.

questions.q: Leptin resistance in obese individuals means:
options: Leptin levels are low despite high fat mass
More leptin is produced but tissues are less responsive, so even more leptin is needed
Leptin receptors are absent from adipose tissue
Leptin promotes appetite in obese individuals
The ob gene is mutated in most obese people
questions.explain: Obese individuals often have HIGH leptin levels (due to more fat tissue producing it) but show leptin resistance — tissues respond less to it. This is analogous to insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes. The obese state resembles perceived starvation at the receptor level.

questions.q: Which thyroid action does NOT occur in the brain?
options: Development of neural tissue requiring GH presence
questions.explain: Thyroid hormones stimulate cellular respiration (increasing basal metabolic rate) in ALL tissues EXCEPT the brain, spleen, testes/uterus, and thyroid itself. This is a key exam trap.

questions.q: Which of the following is TRUE regarding reproductive hormones?
options: All reproductive hormones are secreted from sex organs
Androgens are present only in males
questions.explain: Reproductive hormones include steroids (testosterone, estrogen, progesterone) and peptides (oxytocin, prolactin). Not all steroids are reproductive hormones (e.g., cortisol, aldosterone). Androgens are present in both sexes.

questions.q: The biosynthetic pathway of sex steroid hormones is:
questions.explain: All sex steroids derive from cholesterol. The biosynthetic pathway goes: cholesterol -> progesterone -> androgens (e.g., testosterone) -> estrogens (e.g., estradiol). This is important for understanding hormone interconversions.

questions.q: Spermatogenesis occurs in:
questions.explain: Spermatogenesis (sperm production) occurs in the seminiferous tubules. Leydig cells (interstitial cells) produce testosterone. Sertoli cells support developing sperm. The epididymis is where final sperm maturation occurs.

questions.q: Leydig cells respond to which pituitary hormone?
questions.explain: Leydig cells (interstitial cells of the testis) respond to LH (luteinizing hormone) and produce testosterone. FSH binds to Sertoli cells and stimulates spermatogenesis. This distinction is a common exam trap.

questions.q: Which is FALSE about Leydig cells?
options: They are the target of LH
They are outside the seminiferous tubules
They are also called interstitial cells
questions.explain: Inhibin is secreted by SERTOLI cells, not Leydig cells. Leydig cells secrete testosterone (androgens) in response to LH, and are located outside (interstitial to) the seminiferous tubules.

questions.q: Sertoli cells perform all of the following functions EXCEPT:
questions.explain: Sertoli cells bind FSH, not LH. They nourish germ cells, produce seminiferous tubular fluid, secrete inhibin (which inhibits FSH), and form the blood-testes barrier. LH acts on Leydig cells to produce testosterone.

questions.q: Inhibin from Sertoli cells provides negative feedback on:
questions.explain: Inhibin from Sertoli cells specifically inhibits FSH release from the anterior pituitary. Testosterone provides negative feedback on GnRH, LH, and FSH. This allows independent regulation of sperm production (FSH) and testosterone (LH).

questions.q: Prolonged aerobic exercise inhibits GnRH secretion in both males and females. This results in:
options: Increased sperm production in males only
Inhibition of sperm production in males and disruption of menstruation in females
questions.explain: Stress from protracted aerobic exercise inhibits GnRH secretion from the hypothalamus. In males this reduces LH and FSH, decreasing spermatogenesis. In females it disrupts the menstrual cycle, causing amenorrhea (absent periods).

questions.q: Testosterone is secreted by:
options: Both Sertoli and Leydig cells equally
questions.explain: Testosterone is primarily secreted by Leydig cells in the testis. Small amounts are also produced by the adrenal cortex (as DHEA). Sertoli cells do not produce testosterone — they require it locally (from Leydig cells) for spermatogenesis.

questions.q: In prenatal male development, testosterone triggers:
options: Development of primary male sex characteristics (genitalia)
questions.explain: In prenatal development, testosterone from fetal Leydig cells triggers development of primary male sex characteristics (penis, scrotum, testes). Secondary sex characteristics (voice, muscle, facial hair) develop at puberty under testosterone stimulation.

questions.q: Fertilization of the ovum normally occurs in:
questions.explain: Fertilization occurs in the fallopian tube (oviduct/uterine tube), not in the uterus. The ovum is transported from the ovary to the uterus via the fallopian tube, and sperm fertilize it there. The fertilized ovum then implants in the endometrium.

questions.q: The corpus luteum forms from:
options: A new follicle stimulated by FSH
questions.explain: After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone and estrogen during the luteal phase. If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates, causing progesterone/estrogen to fall and triggering menstruation.

questions.q: In the follicular phase (day 0-12), rising estrogen levels from the growing follicle provide:
options: Positive feedback to hypothalamus, increasing GnRH and triggering LH/FSH surge
Direct inhibition of FSH release from the start
questions.explain: During days 10-14, increasing estrogen from the growing follicle provides positive feedback to the hypothalamus, triggering an LH (and FSH) surge around day 12-14 that causes ovulation. This is a key distinction from the luteal phase, where estrogen/progesterone provide negative feedback.

questions.q: The LH surge at mid-cycle triggers:
questions.explain: The LH surge (along with FSH surge) at around day 14 triggers ovulation — release of the mature ovum from the dominant follicle. After ovulation, the follicle becomes the corpus luteum.

questions.q: Human chorionic gonadotropin (CG) from the placenta serves to:
options: Rescue the corpus luteum and prevent its degeneration
questions.explain: If fertilization and implantation occur, the placenta secretes chorionic gonadotropin (CG) within a day. CG rescues the corpus luteum from degeneration, maintaining progesterone and estrogen production through the first trimester.

questions.q: Ovulation can be prevented by:
options: High estrogen and progesterone (as in contraceptive pills)
questions.explain: High levels of estrogen and progesterone (as in the luteal phase or from contraceptive pills) provide negative feedback to the hypothalamus, suppressing GnRH and thus LH/FSH secretion, preventing ovulation.

questions.q: Contraceptive pills prevent pregnancy by:
options: Suppressing gonadotropin release (preventing ovulation) and increasing cervical mucus viscosity
Stimulating estrogen receptors in the endometrium
questions.explain: Combined oral contraceptives (estrogen + progestogen) prevent ovulation by suppressing LH/FSH release via negative feedback on the hypothalamus/pituitary. They also increase cervical mucus viscosity to inhibit sperm mobility.

questions.q: Gestational diabetes is primarily caused by:
options: High estrogen during pregnancy causing insulin resistance in peripheral tissues
Increased glucose absorption in the GI tract
questions.explain: As the placenta grows, it produces increasing amounts of estrogen, which decreases the sensitivity of peripheral tissues to insulin. When the pancreas cannot compensate by producing enough extra insulin, gestational diabetes results. It typically develops around 20-24 weeks.

questions.q: Effects of estrogen at puberty include all EXCEPT:
options: Stimulating growth of long bones and early closure of epiphyses
Directly increasing insulin sensitivity of peripheral tissues
questions.explain: Estrogen DECREASES (not increases) the sensitivity of peripheral tissues to insulin. This is why gestational diabetes can occur. All other options are correct effects of estrogen at puberty.

questions.q: The function of the oviducts is:
options: Site of fertilization and transport of ovum from ovary to uterus
questions.explain: The oviducts (fallopian tubes/uterine tubes) are the site of fertilization and transport the ovum from the ovary to the uterus. Estrogen is produced in the ovary's follicles. The uterus is the site of implantation.

questions.q: Progesterone acts to:
options: Prevent ovulation during pregnancy, stimulate endometrial development, and develop mammary glands
questions.explain: Progesterone stimulates full endometrial development for pregnancy, further develops mammary glands, and provides negative feedback to prevent further ovulation during pregnancy. It also antagonizes testosterone effects in females.

questions.q: About 400-500 mature ova will be produced during a woman's reproductive lifespan because:
options: One ovum is expelled per ovulatory cycle (~monthly) from puberty to menopause
Both ovaries release one egg each cycle
Oogenesis continues throughout adulthood at a steady rate
questions.explain: One ovum is released per monthly ovulatory cycle. Over a reproductive lifespan of roughly 30-35 years (puberty to menopause), approximately 400-500 ova are produced total — far fewer than the millions of sperm produced daily.

questions.q: IVF (in vitro fertilization) routinely requires ovarian hyperstimulation. Which hormone is used to stimulate multiple egg development?
questions.explain: IVF requires FSH to stimulate multiple follicle development (ovarian hyperstimulation). Final oocyte maturation induction uses human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG). Robert Edwards (Nobel Prize 2010) and Patrick Steptoe pioneered IVF.

questions.q: Which statement about the female reproductive cycle is FALSE?
options: LH secretion is inhibited by estrogen and progesterone in the luteal phase
Corpus luteum secretes estradiol and progesterone
FSH release is partly responsible for follicular maturation
The corpus luteum does not regress if chorionic gonadotropins are produced
Ovulation is prevented by follicular estrogen secretion
questions.explain: Ovulation is not prevented by follicular estrogen — in fact, rising estrogen from the follicle provides POSITIVE feedback causing the LH surge that TRIGGERS ovulation. Ovulation is prevented during the luteal phase by high progesterone/estrogen (negative feedback).

questions.q: In males, testosterone provides negative feedback on:
questions.explain: Testosterone provides negative feedback on all three levels: GnRH (hypothalamus), LH, and FSH (anterior pituitary). Inhibin from Sertoli cells specifically inhibits FSH. This multi-level feedback allows fine regulation of spermatogenesis.

questions.q: Primary sex characteristics in males include:
questions.explain: Primary sex characteristics are directly related to reproduction and include the genitalia. Secondary sex characteristics (voice, facial hair, muscle mass, breast development) are other distinguishing features not directly reproductive.

questions.q: The scrotum houses testes outside the abdominal cavity because:
options: Sperm production does not occur at normal body temperature and requires cooler temperature
To protect the testes from intra-abdominal pressure
questions.explain: Sperm production (spermatogenesis) requires a temperature slightly below normal body temperature (37°C). The scrotum keeps testes at approximately 34°C, which is optimal for sperm development.

questions.q: Sperm count below which level markedly impairs male fertility?
questions.explain: If sperm count falls below 35 million/mL, fertility is markedly impaired. The average college-age male produces about 60 million sperm/mL semen. Normal human fertility thresholds are around 50 million/mL.

questions.q: Which two peptide hormones from the pituitary function in childbirth and lactation?
questions.explain: Oxytocin (from the posterior pituitary) stimulates uterine contractions during childbirth and milk ejection during nursing. Prolactin (from the anterior pituitary) stimulates milk protein synthesis. Both are essential for successful lactation.

questions.q: Menstruation (sloughing of endometrium) is triggered by:
options: Fall in progesterone and estrogen as corpus luteum degenerates
Rising FSH at start of new cycle
questions.explain: When the corpus luteum degenerates (in absence of implantation), progesterone and estrogen levels fall. This fall removes the support for the endometrium, triggering menstrual bleeding (sloughing). Menstruation lasts approximately 5 days.

lectures.title: GI1: Overview of the Digestive System

questions.q: The digestive system is responsible for:
options: Ingestion, digestion, and absorption of food
questions.explain: The digestive system (alimentary canal + accessory digestive organs) is responsible for ingestion, digestion, and absorption of food. It also absorbs water and eliminates waste.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of MECHANICAL digestion?
options: Enzyme cleavage of proteins to peptides
Pepsin digesting proteins in the stomach
questions.explain: Mechanical digestion involves physical breakdown: chewing, mixing, churning, and segmentation (rhythmic local constrictions of the intestine). Chemical digestion uses enzymes to break down molecules.

questions.q: Carbohydrates are ultimately broken down into:
questions.explain: Carbohydrates (starch, disaccharides) are broken down to monosaccharides — glucose, fructose, and galactose — which are then absorbed into the bloodstream. Proteins become amino acids; fats become glycerol and fatty acids.

questions.q: Which segment of the GI tract is the major site of chemical digestion and nutrient absorption?
questions.explain: The small intestine (~6 m long) is the primary site of both chemical digestion and nutrient absorption. The stomach is mainly for storage and partial digestion. The large intestine absorbs water and ions.

questions.q: The large intestine primarily functions to:
options: Accumulate waste and absorb water and ions
questions.explain: The large intestine (hindgut) absorbs water, ions, and some vitamins from undigested material, converting it into feces. The rectum and anus expel the feces. There is no significant nutrient digestion in the large intestine.

questions.q: Which region of the GI tract is classified as 'foregut'?
questions.explain: The foregut includes the esophagus and stomach: food conduction, storage, and digestion. The headgut is the mouth/pharynx (intake and primary processing). The midgut is the small intestine (digestion/absorption). The hindgut is the large intestine (water absorption/excretion).

questions.q: The enteric nervous system contains approximately how many neurons?
questions.explain: The enteric nervous system (ENS) — the 'little brain in the gut' — contains approximately 100 million neurons, more than a mouse brain. It includes sensory neurons, interneurons, and motor neurons organized as an independent integrative system.

questions.q: The enteric nervous system consists of which two plexuses?
questions.explain: The enteric nervous system consists of the myenteric plexus (between muscle layers, controls motility) and the submucosal plexus (controls secretion and absorption). Together they form an independent integrative system in the gut wall.

questions.q: Peristalsis is characterized by:
options: Asynchronous constriction without longitudinal muscle involvement
A traveling wave of constriction in an aboral direction, mainly for propulsion
Segmentation of food into smaller particles
Random mixing without net food movement
Occurring only in the large intestine
questions.explain: Peristalsis is a traveling wave of constriction (involving both longitudinal and circular smooth muscles) that moves food in an aboral direction. It is the main propulsive mechanism and occurs in the esophagus, distal stomach, small intestine, and large intestine.

questions.q: Segmentation differs from peristalsis in that segmentation:
options: Is asynchronous contraction without longitudinal muscle, mainly for mixing
Moves food in an oral direction
questions.explain: Segmentation involves asynchronous constrictions of the gut tube without the longitudinal muscle layer. Its main function is mechanical breakdown and mixing of food, not net propulsion. It occurs in the small and large intestines.

questions.q: Intrinsic control of GI motility is mediated by:
questions.explain: Intrinsic (local) control of GI motility is mediated by the enteric nervous system, modulated by stretching of the GI wall and substances in the chyme. Extrinsic control involves GI peptide hormones and the sympathetic/parasympathetic nervous systems.

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation of the GI tract is generally:
options: Inhibitory only for the small intestine
Has no effect on GI function
questions.explain: Sympathetic innervation is generally inhibitory to GI motility and secretion (fight-or-flight response reduces digestive activity). Parasympathetic stimulation is excitatory, increasing GI motility and secretion.

questions.q: Hirschsprung's disease results from:
options: Failure of nerves to form in all or part of the large intestine
Peptic ulcer disease of the colon
questions.explain: In Hirschsprung's disease, enteric nerves fail to develop in part or all of the large intestine. Waste cannot pass through the non-innervated segment, causing the normal colon above to swell with blocked stool.

questions.q: The microscopic layers of the GI tract from inside to outside are:
questions.explain: From inside (lumen) to outside: mucosa (epithelial/connective/smooth muscle), submucosa (glands/connective tissue), muscularis (longitudinal and circular muscle fibers), serosa (visceral peritoneum).

questions.q: The muscularis layer of the GI tract consists of:
options: Both longitudinal and circular smooth muscle fibers
questions.explain: The muscularis contains both longitudinal and circular smooth muscle fiber layers. Their coordinated contractions produce peristalsis and segmentation. The upper esophagus and external anal sphincter contain skeletal muscle.

questions.q: Food moves down the GI tract:
options: By muscular peristalsis, independent of gravity
Primarily by segmentation pushing food downward
questions.explain: GI motility (peristalsis) can move food in the absence of gravity, as demonstrated by the fact that people can swallow and digest food when lying down or even upside down. Gravity assists but is not essential.

questions.q: The initiation of peristalsis results mainly from:
questions.explain: Peristalsis is mainly initiated by distension (stretching) of the gut wall from food contents. Chemoreceptors responding to chyme composition also contribute. The ENS integrates these signals to coordinate peristaltic waves.

questions.q: GI peptide hormones are an example of:
questions.explain: GI peptide hormones (like gastrin, secretin, GIP) are part of the extrinsic hormonal control of GI motility and secretion. They are secreted by enteroendocrine cells in response to chemical stimulants in the lumen.

questions.q: The main function of the stomach is:
options: Complete digestion and absorption of nutrients
Food reservoir, mechanical mixing, partial digestion, and controlled emptying into the duodenum
questions.explain: The stomach serves as a reservoir (stores food eaten all at once), mechanically mixes food with gastric secretions to form chyme, initiates partial chemical digestion, and empties chyme at a controlled rate into the duodenum.

questions.q: The pancreas contributes to digestion through which type of secretion?
options: Both endocrine (1% of gland) and exocrine (99% of gland)
questions.explain: The pancreas has both endocrine function (1% of the gland: islets of Langerhans producing insulin and glucagon) and exocrine function (99%: acinar cells and duct cells producing digestive enzymes and NaHCO3-rich juice).

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the liver in the GI system?
options: Producing digestive enzymes like amylase and lipase
Processing absorbed nutrients from the portal vein
questions.explain: The liver does NOT produce amylase or lipase — those are from the pancreas and salivary glands. The liver produces bile (its major exocrine function) and controls overall body metabolism (its non-digestive function).

questions.q: The portal vein carries blood from:
options: The digestive organs, spleen, pancreas, and gallbladder to the liver
The small intestine directly to the heart
The liver to the systemic circulation
questions.explain: The portal vein collects blood from the digestive organs, spleen, pancreas, and gallbladder and delivers it to the liver first. This means absorbed nutrients go to the liver before entering general circulation — important for first-pass metabolism.

questions.q: Which type of GI motility is primarily responsible for propulsion of food?
questions.explain: Peristalsis is the primary propulsive mechanism of the GI tract. Segmentation is primarily for mixing. Both involve circular muscle, but only peristalsis uses coordinated longitudinal + circular muscle contractions for net movement.

questions.q: The serosa of the GI tract is also known as:
questions.explain: The serosa is the outermost layer of the GI tract and is also called the visceral peritoneum. It is a serous membrane that reduces friction between the GI tract and surrounding organs in the abdominal cavity.

questions.q: The accessory digestive organs include:
questions.explain: The accessory digestive organs — pancreas, liver, and gallbladder — contribute secretions to the digestive process but are not part of the alimentary canal through which food passes.

questions.q: Which of the following controls the rate at which stomach contents empty into the small intestine?
questions.explain: The pyloric sphincter (at the pylorus of the stomach) controls the rate of stomach emptying into the duodenum. This controlled release is important for proper digestion — too fast would overwhelm the duodenum's buffering capacity.

questions.q: Food can move through the GI tract in the absence of gravity because:
options: Peristaltic contractions of smooth muscle propel food actively
questions.explain: Peristaltic contractions of the smooth muscle layers actively propel food through the GI tract, independent of gravity. This is why astronauts can digest food in space and why people can drink when lying down.

questions.q: Extrinsic neural control of the GI tract involves:
options: The autonomic system only at the stomach
questions.explain: Extrinsic control of GI motility and secretion involves the sympathetic nervous system (generally inhibitory) and parasympathetic nervous system (generally excitatory). This extrinsic control modulates the intrinsic ENS activity.

questions.q: Which statement about the GI tract structure is TRUE?
options: The mucosa contains primarily muscle tissue
The submucosa contains glands and loose connective tissue
The muscularis is made of cardiac muscle
The serosa is the innermost layer
The mucosa is the outermost layer
questions.explain: The submucosa contains glands and loose connective tissue, as well as the submucosal plexus of the ENS. The mucosa is the innermost layer (not outermost), and the muscularis contains smooth muscle (not cardiac).

questions.q: The 'little brain in the gut' refers to:
options: The hypothalamic control center for digestion
The vagal nerve nuclei in the brainstem
questions.explain: The enteric nervous system (ENS) is nicknamed the 'little brain in the gut' because it contains about 100 million neurons organized as an independent integrative system with sensory, interneurons, and motor neurons — capable of autonomous reflex activity.

lectures.title: GI2: Digestion — Mouth, Esophagus, Stomach, Pancreas & Liver

questions.q: The daily volume of saliva produced is approximately:
questions.explain: Salivary glands produce approximately 1.5 L of saliva per day. Total GI secretions are 7-10 L/day. The water lost in feces is only ~100 mL/day, meaning the GI tract reabsorbs most of this fluid.

questions.q: Which enzyme in saliva initiates carbohydrate digestion?
questions.explain: Salivary amylase begins chemical digestion of starches in the mouth, breaking them into maltose, maltotriose, and oligosaccharides. It is released by serous cells of the salivary glands.

questions.q: Saliva is slightly alkaline because it:
options: Contains NaHCO3 which neutralizes acids from oral bacteria and refluxed stomach acid
questions.explain: Saliva is slightly alkaline and helps neutralize acids produced by oral bacteria (preventing caries) and acid refluxed from the stomach. Saliva also contains antibacterial proteins (peroxidase, lysozyme, defensins).

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation of salivary glands produces:
options: A smaller volume of more viscous, mucin-rich saliva, causing dry mouth sensation
questions.explain: Sympathetic stimulation causes secretion of viscous, mucin-rich saliva and is less effective than parasympathetic stimulation. This is why anxiety (sympathetic activation) causes dry mouth. Parasympathetic stimulation produces a moderate volume of watery saliva.

questions.q: Defensins in saliva are:
options: Amphipathic cationic peptides that disrupt pathogen membranes and block viral receptors
questions.explain: Defensins are amphipathic cationic antimicrobial peptides secreted by immune cells and epithelial cells. They disrupt pathogen membranes, block viral receptors, and contribute to the antibacterial function of saliva.

questions.q: The epiglottis moves over the glottis during swallowing to:
options: Close off the larynx and trachea to prevent aspiration of food
Push food into the esophagus by direct contact
Allow nasopharynx to connect with the oropharynx
questions.explain: During the swallowing reflex, the epiglottis closes over the glottis (vocal cords) to seal off the larynx and trachea, preventing food from entering the airway. The soft palate simultaneously closes the nasal passage.

questions.q: GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) results from:
options: Failure of the lower esophageal sphincter to close after food enters the stomach
Hypersecretion of mucus in the esophagus
Esophageal peristalsis that is too fast
Failure of the upper esophageal sphincter
questions.explain: GERD occurs when the lower esophageal (cardiac) sphincter fails to close properly, allowing acidic stomach contents to reflux into the esophagus, causing 'heartburn.' It affects 10-20% of Western populations.

questions.q: Gastrin is secreted by G cells in the stomach antrum and stimulates:
options: Gastric acid (HCl) secretion from parietal cells, gastric gland growth, and gastric motility
questions.explain: Gastrin is released from G cells when the stomach is distended and when proteins are being digested. It primarily stimulates HCl release from parietal cells, promotes growth of gastric gland mucosa, and increases gastric motility.

questions.q: The three major stimuli for gastric acid secretion act through:
options: Separate independent pathways with additive effects
Synergistic pathways all ultimately stimulating H+/K+ ATPase through cAMP or Ca++ signaling
Completely separate mechanisms with no interaction
questions.explain: Histamine (paracrine), acetylcholine (neural), and gastrin (endocrine) all stimulate parietal cell H+/K+ ATPase through cAMP or Ca++ signaling. Importantly, these three stimuli are SYNERGISTIC, which is why blocking one (e.g., H2 blockers) can significantly reduce acid secretion.

questions.q: Cimetidine reduces heartburn by:
options: Blocking histamine receptors on parietal cells
questions.explain: Cimetidine (an H2 blocker) blocks histamine receptors on parietal cells. Since histamine is one of three synergistic stimuli for acid secretion, blocking it significantly reduces HCl production. It is one of the most widely used medicines globally.

questions.q: Pepsinogen is converted to active pepsin when stomach pH is:
questions.explain: Pepsinogen (inactive) is converted to pepsin when pH falls below 5. Pepsin is a protease most active at pH ~2. Once formed, pepsin can autocatalytically convert more pepsinogen to pepsin (positive feedback).

questions.q: Intrinsic factor from parietal cells is essential for absorption of:
questions.explain: Intrinsic factor is a glycoprotein from parietal cells that binds vitamin B12 (cobalamin), which is needed for RBC production. The intrinsic factor-B12 complex is absorbed in the ileum. Without intrinsic factor, pernicious anemia results.

questions.q: Helicobacter pylori survives in the stomach by:
options: Producing urease to neutralize local acidity
Living only in the mucus layer at pH 7
questions.explain: H. pylori produces urease, which converts urea to ammonia, neutralizing local acid and creating a microenvironment suitable for survival. It colonizes about 50% of the world population and increases risk of gastric ulcers.

questions.q: The cephalic phase of gastric secretion is triggered by:
options: Sight, smell, taste, or thought of food
questions.explain: The cephalic phase (nervous system control) is initiated by sight, smell, taste, or thought of food, and by chewing and swallowing. It prepares the stomach for incoming food via vagal (parasympathetic) stimulation.

questions.q: The three phases of gastric secretion in order are:
questions.explain: Gastric secretion occurs in three phases: (1) Cephalic phase (sight/smell/thought of food), (2) Gastric phase (food entering stomach — distension and amino acids), (3) Intestinal phase (food entering intestine).

questions.q: Exocrine pancreatic acinar cells secrete:
options: A large volume of NaHCO3-rich fluid
Inactive digestive enzyme zymogens in a small volume of juice
Active enzymes directly into the intestinal lumen
questions.explain: Acinar cells secrete a small volume of juice containing inactive digestive enzymes (zymogens). Duct epithelial cells secrete the large volume of NaHCO3-rich watery solution. Together these form pancreatic juice (1-2 L/day).

questions.q: Pancreatic zymogens are activated in the duodenum by:
options: Enteropeptidase converting trypsinogen to trypsin, which then activates other zymogens
questions.explain: Enteropeptidase (a transmembrane protein in the brush border of the duodenum/jejunum) converts trypsinogen to trypsin. Trypsin then activates all other pancreatic zymogens. This cascade in the intestinal lumen prevents autodigestion of the pancreas.

questions.q: The NaHCO3-rich pancreatic duct secretion serves to:
options: Neutralize acidic gastric chyme entering the duodenum and provide alkaline pH for pancreatic enzymes
questions.explain: Duct epithelial cells of the pancreas secrete NaHCO3-rich fluid that neutralizes the acidic chyme from the stomach as it enters the duodenum, creating an alkaline pH (~7-8) optimal for pancreatic enzyme activity.

questions.q: Bile salts are amphipathic and function to:
options: Directly digest fats using enzyme activity
Emulsify fats into smaller droplets and solubilize cholesterol and phospholipids in bile
questions.explain: Bile salts are amphipathic molecules (synthesized from cholesterol in the liver) that emulsify large fat globules into smaller droplets (increasing surface area for lipase), and solubilize cholesterol and phospholipids within bile (preventing gallstones).

questions.q: Jaundice results from:
options: High levels of bilirubin (bile pigment) in the blood
questions.explain: Jaundice (yellowing of skin and eyes) results from high levels of bilirubin in the blood. Bilirubin is a breakdown product of hemoglobin that gives bile its yellow-green color. It rises when liver function is impaired or bile ducts are blocked.

questions.q: Gallstones are most commonly:
options: Cholesterol stones caused by decreased bile salt secretion or increased cholesterol synthesis
questions.explain: Cholesterol gallstones form when the balance between cholesterol, bile salts, and phospholipids in bile is disrupted — either from decreased bile salt secretion or increased cholesterol synthesis. Bile salts normally keep cholesterol in solution.

questions.q: When food is not being digested, bile is:
options: Stored and concentrated in the gallbladder via water removal
questions.explain: Between meals, the sphincter of Oddi is contracted, diverting bile through the cystic duct into the gallbladder. The gallbladder stores and concentrates bile by removing water. During a meal, the gallbladder contracts and releases bile into the duodenum.

questions.q: Which is NOT a component of gastric juice?
questions.explain: Gastric juice contains mucus (from mucous cells), pepsinogen and gastric lipase (from chief cells), HCl and intrinsic factor (from parietal cells), and gastrin (from G cells). Amylase is from salivary glands and pancreas, NOT the stomach.

questions.q: Gastric lipase accounts for what percentage of triglyceride digestion?
questions.explain: Gastric lipase initiates fat digestion in the stomach, accounting for 10-30% of triglyceride digestion. It breaks triglycerides into diglycerides and fatty acids. The majority of fat digestion is completed by pancreatic lipase in the small intestine.

questions.q: The rugae of the stomach serve to:
options: Enable expansion/distension of the empty stomach
questions.explain: Rugae are large mucosal folds in the empty stomach that allow the stomach to expand significantly when food enters, accommodating its role as a food reservoir. They flatten out when the stomach is full.

questions.q: Parietal cell HCl secretion involves which three transport mechanisms?
options: Na+/K+ ATPase, Na+/H+ exchanger, K+ channels
H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump), Cl-/HCO3- exchanger, Cl- channels
H+/Na+ exchanger, K+ channels, Ca++ pump
Na+ channels, K+ channels, Cl-/HCO3- exchanger
H+/K+ ATPase, Na+/K+ ATPase, Ca++ channels
questions.explain: Parietal cells secrete HCl using: (1) H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on apical membrane, (2) Cl-/HCO3- exchanger on basolateral membrane (the HCO3- from carbonic anhydrase is exchanged for Cl-), (3) Cl- channel on apical membrane releasing Cl-.

questions.q: The mouth's three major structures and their primary digestive functions are:
options: Teeth (grinding), tongue (manipulation and swallowing), salivary glands (saliva production)
questions.explain: The three major structures of the mouth: Teeth (biting, tearing, grinding — mechanical digestion), Tongue (tasting, manipulating food bolus, involved in swallowing), Salivary glands (secreting multifunctional saliva containing amylase, mucins, and antibacterial proteins).

questions.q: The nasopharynx is:
options: A common passageway for food and air
Only for air — food cannot pass through it
Only for food — air cannot pass through it
Part of the lower respiratory tract
questions.explain: The nasopharynx is only for air — it is not a common passageway for food. The oropharynx and laryngopharynx are common passages for both food and air. During swallowing, the soft palate closes off the nasopharynx to prevent food from entering.

questions.q: Mucus in the stomach protects the lining by:
options: Neutralizing HCl chemically throughout the lumen
Maintaining a HCO3- gradient so the cell surface pH is near neutral even though lumen pH is ~2
Forming a physical barrier only without buffering
Stimulating bicarbonate secretion from chief cells
questions.explain: Gastric mucus (from surface mucous cells) contains HCO3- that maintains a pH gradient: the stomach lumen is ~pH 2, but the cell surface is kept near neutral pH. This protects the epithelium from self-digestion by its own acid and pepsin.

questions.q: Which statement about pancreatic juice is TRUE?
options: It is secreted by islet cells
It contains nearly all enzymes needed for nutrient digestion and is produced at 1-2 L/day
It is strongly acidic to activate zymogens
It is produced only by acinar cells
questions.explain: Pancreatic juice contains almost all digestive enzymes (proteases, amylase, lipase, phospholipase, nucleases) and is produced at 1-2 L/day. It is alkaline (due to duct cell NaHCO3 secretion), and is produced by both acinar cells and duct epithelial cells.

lectures.title: GI3: Absorption — Small Intestine & Large Intestine

questions.q: Absorption is defined as:
options: Transport of molecules through epithelial cells lining the GI tract into blood or lymph
Storage of nutrients in the liver
questions.explain: Absorption is the process by which digested molecules are transported through the epithelial cells lining the GI tract and then enter the blood or lymph. It occurs only after complete chemical digestion and primarily in the small intestine.

questions.q: The true absorptive surface area of the small intestine is approximately:
questions.explain: While the gross surface of the SI is ~0.33 m2, the true absorptive surface reaches ~200 m2 (a doubles tennis court) due to three amplifying structures: circular folds (3x), villi (10x), and microvilli (20x).

questions.q: Which three structures amplify the absorptive surface area of the small intestine?
questions.explain: The small intestine surface is amplified by: circular folds (10mm ridges, 3x increase), villi (0.5-1mm finger-like projections, 10x increase), and microvilli on absorptive cells (20x increase). Rugae are in the STOMACH, not the small intestine.

questions.q: Which monosaccharides require active transport (via SGLT1) for absorption?
options: Glucose and galactose (via SGLT1); fructose is absorbed passively
questions.explain: Glucose and galactose are absorbed by SGLT1 (sodium-glucose transporter 1) using secondary active transport (Na+ gradient drives uphill glucose/galactose uptake). Fructose is absorbed passively. This is why sodium in ORT solutions enhances glucose absorption.

questions.q: Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) works because:
options: Glucose directly osmotically draws water into the gut
Glucose absorption via SGLT1 cotransports Na+, and water follows salt absorption
NaCl alone is sufficient without glucose
questions.explain: ORT uses glucose to enhance Na+ uptake via SGLT1 (discovered by Robert Crane). Water then follows the osmotic gradient created by salt absorption. This simple solution reduced diarrheal deaths by 93% and is on the WHO Essential Medicines list.

questions.q: Lactose intolerance symptoms occur because:
options: Undigested lactose remains in the intestine, causing osmotic fluid accumulation and bacterial fermentation producing gas
Lactose directly irritates the intestinal lining
Lactose increases intestinal motility by a neural reflex
questions.explain: Lactose intolerance results from insufficient lactase. Undigested lactose remains in the intestine: (a) draws fluid by osmosis causing diarrhea, and (b) is fermented by colonic bacteria producing lactic acid, CO2, H2, and methane causing gas, bloating, and cramps.

questions.q: The primary pathway for protein absorption after digestion is:
options: Free amino acids via Na+-dependent transporters
Dipeptides and tripeptides via H+-driven active transporter — the major pathway — then converted to AA intracellularly
Via portal vein as peptides directly
questions.explain: Dipeptides and tripeptides are the MAJOR pathway for amino acid absorption — they are actively transported by an H+-coupled transporter (PepT1) and converted to amino acids intracellularly. Free amino acid absorption is slower and uses Na+-dependent transporters.

questions.q: Long-chain fatty acids (>12 carbons) are absorbed and enter circulation via:
options: Portal vein directly as free fatty acids
Chylomicrons expelled by exocytosis into the central lacteal (lymphatic system)
questions.explain: Long-chain fatty acids and monoglycerides are resynthesized into triglycerides in the ER, packaged with other lipids and apolipoproteins into chylomicrons (100-500nm) in the Golgi, and expelled by exocytosis into the central lacteal. They are too large for capillaries so enter the lymph instead.

questions.q: Short-chain fatty acids (<12 carbons) are absorbed by:
options: Direct absorption into the portal vein
questions.explain: Short-chain fatty acids (<12 carbons) are water-soluble enough to be absorbed directly into portal blood capillaries without needing chylomicron formation. Long-chain fatty acids require chylomicrons and lymph transport.

questions.q: Mixed micelles in the intestinal lumen consist of:
options: Bile salts, fatty acids, monoglycerides, cholesterol, and phospholipids — keeping hydrophobic lipids soluble
questions.explain: Mixed micelles are formed by amphipathic bile salts surrounding fatty acids, monoglycerides, cholesterol, and phospholipids. They keep these hydrophobic molecules soluble in the aqueous intestinal environment, replenishing free molecules at the cell surface as they are absorbed.

questions.q: Hydrophobic vitamins (A, D, E, K) are absorbed:
options: Together with lipids in mixed micelles
questions.explain: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are absorbed along with dietary lipids, incorporated into mixed micelles and then into chylomicrons. Hydrophilic vitamins (B complex, C) are absorbed by specific membrane carrier proteins. Vitamin B12 requires intrinsic factor.

questions.q: The Na+/K+ ATPase on the basolateral membrane of intestinal epithelial cells is responsible for:
options: Maintaining the low intracellular Na+ that drives SGLT1 and other Na+-dependent absorption
Transporting amino acids across the apical membrane
questions.explain: The Na+/K+ ATPase on the basolateral membrane actively pumps Na+ out of the cell, maintaining low intracellular Na+. This Na+ gradient provides the driving force for all secondary active transport systems (SGLT1 for glucose/galactose, Na+-AA cotransporters, etc.).

questions.q: Calcium absorption in the small intestine is regulated by:
options: Calcitriol (vitamin D3), which regulates Ca channels, calbindin, and Ca2+ ATPase
Parathyroid hormone directly acting on intestinal cells
questions.explain: Calcitriol (active vitamin D, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3) is the major regulator of intestinal Ca2+ absorption. It regulates all three steps: apical Ca2+ channel, intracellular calbindin (Ca-binding protein), and basolateral Ca2+ ATPase.

questions.q: Water absorption in the small intestine occurs mainly by:
options: Osmosis following the transport of osmotically active solutes (Na+, sugars, amino acids)
questions.explain: Water absorption is driven by osmosis — water follows the osmotic gradient created by active transport of solutes (primarily Na+, glucose, and amino acids). Water can pass through tight junctions or aquaporins. Very little net absorption occurs in the duodenum; most occurs in the jejunum.

questions.q: The duodenum is what percentage of the total small intestine length?
questions.explain: The duodenum is only about 26 cm long, representing approximately 5% of the total small intestine length (~6 m). Despite its short length, it is the major site of chemical digestion — where acid is neutralized by pancreatic bicarbonate and pancreatic enzymes and bile are released.

questions.q: Digestion and absorption of carbohydrates is complete by the end of:
questions.explain: Carbohydrate digestion and absorption is complete by the end of the jejunum (the middle segment of the small intestine). Starch is broken down to monosaccharides by salivary/pancreatic amylase and brush border enzymes, then absorbed.

questions.q: Which enzyme in the brush border converts lactose to galactose and glucose?
questions.explain: Lactase is the brush border enzyme that hydrolyzes lactose into galactose and glucose. Deficiency of lactase causes lactose intolerance. Other brush border enzymes: maltase (maltose->glucose), sucrase (sucrose->fructose+glucose), isomaltase (oligosaccharides->glucose).

questions.q: The intestinal blood from absorption travels first to:
options: The liver via the portal vein
questions.explain: Absorbed nutrients enter intestinal capillaries and travel via the portal vein to the liver first. This is clinically important — the liver processes absorbed nutrients, detoxifies substances, and metabolizes drugs before they reach systemic circulation (first-pass effect).

questions.q: The large intestine functions include all EXCEPT:
options: Secreting mucus to protect the mucosa
Storing feces in the sigmoid colon and rectum until defecation
questions.explain: The large intestine does NOT perform significant nutrient digestion. Its functions are: processing waste into feces (absorbing water/ions from remaining chyme), secreting mucus, and storing feces. Bilirubin decomposition in the large intestine gives feces its characteristic color.

questions.q: The ascending and transverse colons are the sites of:
options: Remaining absorption and secretion activities; water absorption dehydrates content forming solid feces
questions.explain: The ascending and transverse colons are the active absorptive segments — absorbing remaining water and ions to dehydrate the colon content into solid feces. The descending and sigmoid colons are primarily storage sites. Once in the rectum, the defecation reflex is triggered.

questions.q: Fecal color is determined by:
questions.explain: The characteristic brown color of feces comes from decomposition products of bilirubin (a bile pigment, itself a breakdown product of hemoglobin). The large intestine processes these pigments as chyme passes through.

questions.q: The cecum is described as:
options: The first segment of the small intestine
The blind gut — the first part of the large intestine
The segment where the rectum meets the colon
The junction of jejunum and ileum
questions.explain: The cecum is the blind-ended first portion of the large intestine (colon). It receives chyme from the ileum via the ileocecal valve. The appendix is attached to the cecum.

questions.q: Which transporter mediates glucose and galactose uptake across the apical membrane of intestinal epithelial cells?
questions.explain: SGLT1 (discovered by Robert Crane) is the sodium-coupled glucose transporter 1 on the apical (brush border) membrane. It cotransports 2 Na+ with each glucose or galactose molecule. GLUT2 mediates exit at the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion. GLUT5 transports fructose.

questions.q: Daily intestinal water transport capacity is approximately:
questions.explain: The small intestine can transport approximately 18 L/day (about 1 million mmoles) of water. Yet only about 100 mL is lost in feces daily, meaning the GI tract reabsorbs ~7-9 L of fluid secreted by itself and ingested fluids.

questions.q: Vitamin B12 absorption requires intrinsic factor because:
options: B12 is fat-soluble and needs a carrier like bile salts
Intrinsic factor from parietal cells binds B12 and the complex is absorbed by specific receptors in the ileum
B12 is too large to be absorbed without degradation
Intrinsic factor protects B12 from stomach acid only
B12 must be converted to its active form by intrinsic factor
questions.explain: Vitamin B12 (a large, water-soluble vitamin) must bind to intrinsic factor (a glycoprotein from parietal cells) to be absorbed. The B12-intrinsic factor complex is recognized by specific receptors in the ileum for absorption. Deficiency of intrinsic factor causes pernicious anemia.

questions.q: The question 'where does the Na+ in the intestinal lumen come from?' for driving SGLT1 is answered by:
options: Active secretion of Na+ by goblet cells
Na+ from intestinal secretions and dietary Na+ — the same Na+ absorbed at the basolateral side is recycled via secretion
Na+ leaking from blood by passive diffusion
questions.explain: The Na+ in the intestinal lumen comes from intestinal secretions and dietary intake. The Na+ gradient is maintained by the Na+/K+ ATPase on the basolateral side, which pumps Na+ out of the cell; this Na+ is then secreted back into the lumen to drive further SGLT1-mediated absorption.

questions.q: The jejunum accounts for what fraction of the small intestine length and is the primary site of:
questions.explain: The jejunum is approximately 2.5 m (40% of the 6 m small intestine) and is the major site of absorption of most nutrients, completing carbohydrate and protein absorption and beginning fat absorption.

questions.q: After lipid digestion products diffuse into absorptive cells, triglycerides are resynthesized in:
questions.explain: Fatty acids and monoglycerides passively diffuse into absorptive cells and are resynthesized into triglycerides in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (ER). These are then packaged into chylomicrons in the Golgi complex before exocytosis into the lymph.

questions.q: The ileum joins the large intestine and accounts for approximately what length?
questions.explain: The ileum is approximately 3.5 m long, joining the large intestine. It continues absorption of nutrients and is the specific site for B12-intrinsic factor complex absorption. The ileocecal valve controls passage from ileum to cecum.

questions.q: Which is correct about Na+ absorption in the intestine?
options: Crosses the mucosal membrane only via ion channels
Absorbed along the entire intestine by several mechanisms; actively expelled by Na+/K+ ATPase at basolateral membrane
Requires ATP on the apical side
questions.explain: Na+ is absorbed along the entire intestine, with the highest rates in the jejunum. It crosses the apical membrane by various mechanisms (channels, cotransporters, exchangers) down its electrochemical gradient, then is actively pumped out at the basolateral membrane by Na+/K+ ATPase.

lectures.title: Neuro1: Physiology of Nerve Cells — Ion Channels, Membrane Potential & Action Potentials

questions.q: Two distinctive features of ion channels compared to other transport proteins are:
options: They use ATP and transport specific ions only
Very high transport rate (>10^6 ions/sec) and ions pass downhill along electrochemical gradient without energy input
They are found only in neurons and they pump ions uphill
They form dimers and bind hormones
They are always open and non-selective
questions.explain: Ion channels are distinguished from other transporters by: (1) extremely high transport rate (>10^6 ions/sec), and (2) ions pass passively down their electrochemical gradient — they do not require metabolic energy like ATP. Carrier proteins and pumps transport more slowly and often require energy.

questions.q: Which type of membrane transport protein always performs PASSIVE transport?
questions.explain: Ion channels always perform passive transport — ions move downhill along their electrochemical gradient through the channel pore. ATP-driven pumps and cotransporters can perform active transport. Channels cannot transport ions against their electrochemical gradient.

questions.q: Secondary active transport uses:
options: The electrochemical gradient of one ion to drive the uphill transport of another ion
A single specific carrier with no energy requirement
questions.explain: Secondary active transport (coupled carriers) uses the electrochemical gradient of one ion (often Na+, established by Na+/K+ ATPase using ATP) to energize the uphill transport of another molecule. Examples: SGLT1 (glucose), H+-peptide transporter (dipeptides).

questions.q: The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron is approximately:
questions.explain: The resting membrane potential of a typical mammalian neuron is approximately -70 mV (inside negative relative to outside). This is close to the K+ equilibrium potential because the resting membrane is most permeable to K+.

questions.q: Two requirements for building a membrane potential are:
options: A permeable membrane and ion pumps only
A semi-permeable membrane and an ion concentration gradient
A fully permeable membrane and active pumps
Equal ion concentrations and selective channels
questions.explain: A membrane potential requires: (1) a semi-permeable membrane (selectively permeable to some ions), and (2) an ion concentration gradient across the membrane. If the membrane were equally permeable to all ions or concentrations were equal, no potential would develop.

questions.q: The Nernst equation for K+ gives approximately -75 mV for a squid neuron because:
options: K+ concentration is higher inside than outside, and the membrane is selectively permeable to K+
Ca2+ concentration gradient determines resting potential
questions.explain: In squid neurons, K+ is ~400 mM inside vs 20 mM outside. With selective K+ permeability, K+ tends to diffuse out, leaving negative charge inside until the electrical gradient (-75 mV) stops net K+ flow. This K+ equilibrium potential approximates the resting potential.

questions.q: The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation accounts for resting membrane potential by incorporating:
options: Relative permeabilities of K+, Na+, and Cl- (typically pK:pNa:pCl = 1:0.05:0.45 at rest)
questions.explain: The GHK equation accounts for the contributions of all permeable ions to the resting potential, weighted by their relative permeabilities. At rest, pK:pNa:pCl = 1:0.05:0.45, so K+ dominates but Na+ and Cl- make contributions that shift the resting potential away from the pure K+ Nernst potential.

questions.q: During the rising phase (depolarization) of an action potential:
options: K+ channels open and K+ flows in
Na+ channels open and Na+ flows in, driven by both concentration and electrical gradients
K+ channels close and K+ flows out
questions.explain: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open during depolarization. Na+ rushes in driven by BOTH its concentration gradient (high outside) AND the electrical gradient (negative inside). This massive inward Na+ current rapidly depolarizes the membrane toward the Na+ equilibrium potential (+55 mV).

questions.q: The 'all-or-none' principle of action potentials means:
options: Every stimulus generates an action potential
The action potential is always the same amplitude regardless of stimulus strength
Different stimulus strengths produce different AP sizes
Only one action potential can occur per neuron
Action potentials always travel in both directions
questions.explain: Action potentials are 'all-or-none' — once the threshold is reached, the AP is always the same shape and amplitude regardless of stimulus strength. Stimulus intensity is encoded by AP FREQUENCY (more stimuli = more APs per second), not AP size.

questions.q: The threshold for action potential generation requires:
options: Na+ influx must exceed K+ outflux, starting a positive feedback loop of more depolarization -> more Na+ channel opening
Ca2+ influx to depolarize the membrane
questions.explain: Threshold is reached when depolarization is sufficient that Na+ influx exceeds K+ outflux, triggering a positive feedback loop: depolarization -> more voltage-gated Na+ channels open -> more Na+ influx -> more depolarization -> even more channels open.

questions.q: During the absolute refractory period:
options: The threshold is slightly elevated but a second AP is possible
Voltage-gated Na+ channels are inactivated, making a second AP impossible
questions.explain: During the absolute refractory period, voltage-gated Na+ channels are in an inactivated (closed) state and CANNOT reopen regardless of stimulus strength. This prevents a second action potential from occurring, setting an upper limit on AP frequency.

questions.q: Why does an action potential propagate in only ONE direction along an axon?
options: Because of the myelin sheath orientation
Because Na+ channels behind the AP are in the absolute refractory period (inactivated) and cannot reopen
Because K+ channels block backward propagation
Because the axon hillock only generates signals going distally
questions.explain: AP propagation is unidirectional because voltage-gated Na+ channels in the region just behind (upstream of) the AP are in the absolute refractory period (inactivated). The AP can only propagate forward into resting membrane where Na+ channels are available.

questions.q: Saltatory conduction in myelinated axons refers to:
options: Action potentials jumping from one Node of Ranvier to the next, greatly increasing conduction velocity
Continuous conduction along the entire axon membrane
The AP spreading through the myelin sheath
questions.explain: In myelinated axons, the myelin sheath is an electrical insulator. Ionic currents can only flow at the Nodes of Ranvier (gaps in myelin). The AP appears to 'jump' (saltate) from node to node — saltatory conduction — which is much faster and more energy-efficient than continuous conduction.

questions.q: The Na+/K+ ATPase pump is important for long-term maintenance of:
options: The ion concentration gradients that underlie the resting membrane potential
questions.explain: The Na+/K+ ATPase pump (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in per ATP) continuously restores the Na+ and K+ concentration gradients depleted by action potentials. Without it, repeated APs would gradually dissipate the ion gradients, causing the resting membrane potential to collapse over time.

questions.q: Tetrodotoxin (TTX) blocks action potentials by:
questions.explain: Tetrodotoxin (TTX), produced by puffer fish and other animals, blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels. Since Na+ influx through these channels is responsible for the rising phase of the action potential, TTX blocks AP generation and is lethal in high doses.

questions.q: Sub-threshold passive signals in neurons are characterized by:
options: No change in signal amplitude over distance
Signals that regenerate at each point like APs
Amplitude that decays rapidly over distance (length constant), with signal slowing and spreading in all directions
The same speed as action potentials
questions.explain: Sub-threshold (passive/electrotonic) signals decay exponentially over distance (characterized by the length constant), slow down, and spread in all directions. They do NOT regenerate. This is why axons need active propagation (APs) for long-distance signaling.

questions.q: At a chemical synapse, step 3 (release) requires:
options: Binding of neurotransmitter to postsynaptic receptors
Depolarization causing Ca2+ influx, which triggers vesicle fusion and exocytosis of neurotransmitter
ATP hydrolysis directly at the vesicle
