questions.explain: At a chemical synapse, release (step 3) requires: depolarization of the presynaptic terminal -> voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open -> Ca2+ influx -> Ca2+ triggers vesicle-membrane fusion -> exocytosis of neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft.

questions.q: An EPSP (excitatory postsynaptic potential) is:
options: A hyperpolarization of the postsynaptic membrane
A depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane, bringing it closer to threshold
An AP in the postsynaptic cell
questions.explain: An EPSP is a depolarization of the postsynaptic membrane — the membrane potential moves toward (or past) threshold. EPSPs are generated by opening of non-selective cation channels (allowing Na+ influx and/or K+ efflux net depolarization). Multiple EPSPs summate to reach threshold for an AP.

questions.q: An IPSP (inhibitory postsynaptic potential) is generated by:
options: Opening of Cl- channels (Cl- flows in, hyperpolarizing) or K+ channels (K+ flows out, hyperpolarizing)
questions.explain: IPSPs are hyperpolarizations generated when inhibitory neurotransmitters (like GABA or glycine) open Cl- channels (Cl- flows in, making inside more negative) or K+ channels (K+ flows out). This moves the membrane potential AWAY from threshold.

questions.q: The nature of the postsynaptic effect (excitation vs inhibition) is determined by:
options: The type of postsynaptic receptor, not the neurotransmitter itself
The size of the presynaptic action potential
The distance from the synapse to the axon hillock
Whether the synapse is axodendritic or axosomatic
questions.explain: Whether a synapse is excitatory or inhibitory depends on the TYPE OF POSTSYNAPTIC RECEPTOR, not the neurotransmitter. The same neurotransmitter (e.g., ACh, norepinephrine) can be excitatory at one synapse and inhibitory at another, depending on receptor type.

questions.q: Termination of postsynaptic neurotransmitter action occurs by:
options: Only re-uptake into the presynaptic terminal
Diffusion away from the cleft, enzymatic degradation, and/or presynaptic re-uptake — varying by transmitter type
questions.explain: Neurotransmitter action is terminated by: (1) diffusion away (peptide transmitters), (2) enzymatic degradation (e.g., acetylcholinesterase degrades ACh), (3) re-uptake into the presynaptic terminal (monoamines like dopamine, serotonin), or combinations.

questions.q: The patch-clamp technique (Neher and Sackmann, Nobel 1991) allows:
options: Recording of single ion channel activity from membrane patches
Visualization of synaptic vesicles by electron microscopy
Measurement of neurotransmitter release by mass spectrometry
Recording of action potentials only from whole neurons
questions.explain: The patch-clamp technique isolates a small patch of cell membrane and records current through single ion channels, revealing their stochastic (random) opening and closing behavior. Erwin Neher and Bert Sackmann received the Nobel Prize in 1991 for developing this technique.

questions.q: The Na+ equilibrium potential (ENa) in a squid neuron is approximately:
questions.explain: Using the Nernst equation with Na+ concentrations (50 mM inside, 440 mM outside for squid), ENa ≈ +55 mV. The large positive value explains why Na+ influx during an AP drives the membrane potential toward +55 mV during the peak of depolarization.

questions.q: During the repolarization phase of an action potential:
options: Voltage-gated K+ channels open, K+ flows out, repolarizing the membrane; Na+ channels inactivate
The Na+/K+ ATPase immediately restores potentials
questions.explain: Repolarization occurs when voltage-gated K+ channels (slower to open than Na+ channels) open, allowing K+ to flow out down its concentration gradient. Simultaneously, voltage-gated Na+ channels inactivate. Together these restore the negative membrane potential.

questions.q: Afterhyperpolarization (undershoot) following an AP occurs because:
options: K+ channels remain open longer than needed, briefly making the inside more negative than resting potential
questions.explain: After the AP, voltage-gated K+ channels close more slowly than Na+ channels inactivate. During this brief period of continued K+ outflow, the membrane potential overshoots below the resting potential (afterhyperpolarization/undershoot), corresponding to the relative refractory period.

questions.q: Which ion has higher concentration INSIDE the neuron compared to outside?
options: All ions are equal inside and outside
questions.explain: K+ is the major intracellular cation: ~140 mM inside vs ~5 mM outside in mammalian neurons (or 400 mM vs 20 mM in squid). Na+ (high outside), Cl- (high outside), and Ca2+ (high outside) all have higher extracellular concentrations.

questions.q: Integration of synaptic inputs at the axon hillock involves:
options: Spatial and temporal summation of EPSPs and IPSPs; an AP is generated only if net depolarization reaches threshold
The largest EPSP always generating an AP
No integration — each synapse independently generates an AP
questions.explain: The axon hillock integrates (sums) all EPSPs and IPSPs from all synapses, both spatially (simultaneous inputs from multiple synapses) and temporally (rapid sequential inputs from the same synapse). An AP is generated only when the net depolarization at the hillock reaches threshold.

questions.q: Synaptic vesicles contain synthesized neurotransmitter and are stored in:
questions.explain: Neurotransmitters are synthesized in the cell body (or locally in the terminal) and packaged into synaptic vesicles stored in the presynaptic terminal. Upon AP-triggered Ca2+ influx, vesicles fuse with the presynaptic membrane and release transmitter into the synaptic cleft.

questions.q: Ions pass through channels 'downhill' along their electrochemical gradient, which is determined by:
options: Both the concentration gradient AND the membrane potential (electrical gradient)
The density of channels in the membrane
questions.explain: The electrochemical gradient = concentration gradient + membrane potential (electrical gradient). Both must be considered to determine the direction and driving force for ion movement through a channel. For example, Na+ moves in driven by both its concentration gradient and the inside-negative electrical gradient.

questions.q: A cell membrane is permeable to Cl-; intracellular [Cl-] = 10 mM, extracellular [Cl-] = 140 mM. Cl- will flow:
options: Inside to outside, driven by concentration gradient
Outside to inside, driven by concentration gradient, and may be opposed by electrical gradient depending on membrane potential
No flow because Cl- is balanced
Cl- doesn't flow through membrane channels
questions.explain: The concentration gradient drives Cl- from outside (140 mM) to inside (10 mM). However, the electrical gradient (negative inside) opposes this since Cl- is negative and the inside is already negative. The net direction depends on the balance — this is what the Nernst equation for Cl- calculates.

lectures.title: Neuro2: Properties of Sensory Cells and Sensory Systems

questions.q: A sensory receptor is best defined as:
options: A specialized cell (neural or non-neural) that receives a specific environmental stimulus and converts it into an electrical signal the CNS can process
Only pain receptors in the skin
Cells in the thalamus that process information
questions.explain: A sensory receptor is a specialized cell — which may be neural (like a nociceptor) or non-neural (like a cochlear hair cell) — that receives a specific environmental stimulus and converts it (transduction) into an electrical signal the CNS can understand.

questions.q: Which type of receptor responds to O2, pH, and glucose concentrations?
questions.explain: Chemoreceptors respond to chemical stimuli including O2, pH, organic molecules like glucose, and many other substances. Other types: mechanoreceptors (pressure, stretch, vibration, sound), photoreceptors (light), thermoreceptors (temperature), nociceptors (pain).

questions.q: Sensory transduction is the process of:
options: Sending action potentials to the motor cortex
Converting stimulus energy into an electrical signal (generator or receptor potential) that can be processed by the CNS
Transmitting signals from the thalamus to the cortex
Perceiving the quality of a sensation
questions.explain: Sensory transduction is the conversion of stimulus energy (mechanical, chemical, thermal, light) into a membrane potential change (generator potential in neural receptors, receptor potential in non-neural receptors) that the nervous system can process.

questions.q: In Type 1/2 sensory transmission (neural sensory receptors), the sequence is:
options: Stimulus -> receptor potential -> neurotransmitter release -> AP in the next neuron
Stimulus -> generator potential -> action potential in the sensory neuron itself
Stimulus -> depolarization of postsynaptic cell directly
questions.explain: In neural sensory receptors (Type 1 and 2 transmission), the stimulus generates a generator potential in the receptor membrane. If the generator potential reaches threshold, it triggers an action potential in the sensory neuron itself, which then propagates to the CNS.

questions.q: In Type 3 sensory transmission (non-neural receptors, e.g., cochlear hair cells), the sequence is:
options: Stimulus -> receptor potential -> neurotransmitter release -> AP in downstream sensory neuron
Stimulus -> generator potential -> AP in the receptor
Stimulus -> inhibitory potential -> downstream neuron AP
questions.explain: In non-neural receptors (Type 3), the receptor cell generates a receptor potential but cannot itself fire APs. Instead, the receptor potential modulates neurotransmitter release, which generates APs in the downstream (afferent) sensory neuron.

questions.q: Sensory coding for stimulus intensity is represented by:
options: The amplitude of each action potential
The frequency of action potentials (or amount of neurotransmitter released)
The duration of each action potential
The diameter of the sensory neuron
questions.explain: Stimulus intensity is encoded by AP frequency — stronger stimuli generate higher-frequency APs. Duration is encoded by the duration of the AP train. Since APs are all-or-none (fixed amplitude), intensity cannot be coded by AP size.

questions.q: All secondary sensory neurons in somatosensory pathways cross the midline. This means:
options: Both sides of the body are processed in the same hemisphere
Sensations from the left side of the body are processed in the RIGHT cerebral hemisphere
Sensations from the right side are processed in the right hemisphere
Crossing occurs at the level of the thalamus only
Motor and sensory neurons cross at the same level
questions.explain: All secondary sensory neurons cross the midline at some point, so sensations from the LEFT side of the body are processed in the RIGHT cerebral hemisphere. The primary sensory neuron does NOT cross; only the secondary (and sometimes tertiary) neurons cross.

questions.q: The somatosensory pathway involves three levels of neurons in order:
options: Primary sensory neuron -> tertiary sensory neuron in thalamus -> secondary sensory neuron in cortex
Primary sensory neuron -> secondary sensory neuron (crosses midline) -> tertiary sensory neuron in thalamus -> somatosensory cortex
Motor neuron -> sensory neuron -> thalamus -> cortex
questions.explain: The somatosensory pathway: (1) Primary sensory neuron: receptor to spinal cord/brainstem; (2) Secondary sensory neuron: crosses midline; synapses in thalamus; (3) Tertiary sensory neuron: thalamus to somatosensory cortex.

questions.q: In the somatosensory cortex, the size of the cortical representation of a body region is proportional to:
options: The physical size of the body region
The sensitivity of the region to all stimuli (more sensitive = larger cortical area)
The number of muscles in the region
Fixed anatomical organization that never changes
The distance of the region from the brain
questions.explain: The somatosensory cortex maps body regions, but the size of each representation is proportional to the region's sensory sensitivity (number of sensory receptors), not its physical size. Hands and lips have very large cortical representations. The cortical map is also plastic — it can change with use.

questions.q: Piezo2 channels are responsible for:
options: Gentle touch sensation and proprioception ('sixth sense' of body position)
questions.explain: Piezo2 is a mechanosensitive ion channel (piezoelectric channel) that mediates both gentle touch sensation and proprioception (sense of self-movement and body position). Discovery of Piezo channels led to the Nobel Prize in Physiology 2021.

questions.q: The TRPV1 channel is activated by:
options: Noxious heat (>43°C) and capsaicin (from hot chili peppers)
questions.explain: TRPV1 (Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid 1) is activated by noxious heat (>43°C) and capsaicin, the active component in hot chili peppers. This is why spicy food feels 'hot' — TRPV1 cannot distinguish chemical capsaicin from actual heat.

questions.q: The TRPM8 channel is activated by:
options: Cold temperatures (below body temperature) and cooling compounds like menthol and eucalyptol
questions.explain: TRPM8 is a cold receptor activated by temperatures below body temperature and by cooling compounds including menthol (from mint) and eucalyptol (from eucalyptus). TRPM8-deficient mice are insensitive to cold, confirming its role as a cold sensor.

questions.q: Discovery of which channels as temperature and touch sensors led to the 2021 Nobel Prize?
questions.explain: The 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for the discovery of TRPV1 (noxious heat and capsaicin), TRPM8 (cold and menthol), and Piezo channels (touch and proprioception) as the molecular sensors for temperature and touch.

questions.q: Nociceptors activate two different pathways:
options: One to the cerebellum and one to the cortex
Reflexive protective responses integrated at the spinal cord level, and ascending pathways to the cortex for conscious sensation
Both pathways to the thalamus only
One to the motor cortex and one to the somatosensory cortex
One neural and one hormonal pathway
questions.explain: Nociceptors activate: (1) local spinal cord reflex arcs that trigger rapid protective responses (withdrawal reflex) before the signal reaches the brain, and (2) ascending pathways to the cortex where the signal becomes conscious pain perception.

questions.q: Sound waves with amplitude greater than 80 dB can:
options: Have no effect on the inner ear
questions.explain: Sounds above 80 dB can damage cochlear hair cells, which are not regenerated in mammals. This is the basis for noise-induced hearing loss. Normal conversation is ~60 dB. The average human ear hears 20-20,000 Hz most acutely in the 1-3 kHz range.

questions.q: The cochlear inner hair cells (IHCs) versus outer hair cells (OHCs) have what innervation pattern?
options: OHCs receive most afferent fibers (10:1 fiber-to-cell)
IHCs receive most afferent fibers (nearly 10:1 fiber-to-IHC ratio from 30,000+ cochlear nerve fibers)
OHCs have only efferent innervation and IHCs have only afferent
IHCs and OHCs have identical innervation patterns
questions.explain: Despite being outnumbered (3,000 IHCs vs 12,000 OHCs), IHCs receive nearly 10:1 fiber-to-cell innervation from the cochlear nerve (30,000+ total fibers). IHCs are the primary sensory transducers sending auditory info to the brain. OHCs receive sparse afferent innervation but are mainly controlled by efferent fibers from the brain.

questions.q: The role of outer hair cells (OHCs) in the cochlea is:
options: Mechanical amplification through electromotility driven by the protein prestin
Synapsing directly with the auditory cortex
questions.explain: OHCs amplify cochlear mechanical responses through electromotility — the protein prestin causes OHCs to mechanically shorten and elongate in response to voltage changes, amplifying the sound signal. This is the cochlear amplifier, which greatly increases sensitivity and frequency selectivity.

questions.q: Place theory of sound frequency coding (Georg von Bekesy, Nobel 1961) states:
options: Different hair cells code different intensities
Each place along the cochlear basilar membrane corresponds to perception of a specific sound frequency
All frequencies activate the same region of the cochlea
Frequency is coded by AP amplitude
questions.explain: Place theory: different frequencies maximally vibrate different places along the basilar membrane of the cochlea. High frequencies activate the base; low frequencies activate the apex. Each position maps to a specific perceived frequency. Georg von Bekesy won the Nobel Prize in 1961 for this work.

questions.q: Proprioception, the 'sixth sense', is the sense of:
options: Self-movement and body position, mediated by Piezo2 channels
questions.explain: Proprioception is the sense of self-movement and body position. Piezo2 channels in mechanoreceptors in muscles, tendons, and joints mediate proprioception. Without it, one cannot sense limb position without looking, affecting balance and coordination.

questions.q: The four somatosensory modalities are:
options: Hot, cold, deep pressure, and light touch only
questions.explain: The four somatosensory modalities are: (1) Touch, (2) Proprioception (body position/movement), (3) Temperature, (4) Nociception (pain and itching). These are distinct submodalities of the somatosensory system.

questions.q: Prestin is a transmembrane protein in outer hair cells that:
options: Detects sound frequency like a filter
Mechanically contracts and elongates in response to voltage changes (electromotility), driving cochlear amplification
Transduces sound into electrical signals like inner hair cells
Forms the tip links between stereocilia
Releases neurotransmitter at the ribbon synapse
questions.explain: Prestin is the motor protein of OHCs. Voltage changes across the OHC membrane (from mechanotransduction via stereocilia) cause prestin to change shape, mechanically contracting and elongating the cell at acoustic frequencies. This electromotility amplifies basilar membrane vibration by up to 1000-fold.

questions.q: The hearing range of the average human ear is:
options: 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz, most acutely in 1-3 kHz
questions.explain: The human ear can detect sounds from 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz), with most acute hearing in the 1-3 kHz range (important for speech). High-frequency hearing deteriorates with age. Some animals (bats, dogs) have much higher frequency ranges.

questions.q: Timbre (音色) in sound refers to:
options: The quality that distinguishes different instruments even at same pitch and loudness
questions.explain: Timbre is the quality or 'color' of a sound that distinguishes, for example, a violin from a clarinet playing the same note at the same volume. It arises from the complexity (harmonic structure) of the sound wave.

questions.q: Warm receptors respond to temperatures:
options: From body temperature up to ~45°C; TRPV1 responds to noxious heat above 43°C
questions.explain: Warm receptors (including TRPV channels) respond to temperatures from body temperature (~37°C) up to about 45°C. TRPV1 specifically responds to noxious heat above 43°C. Cold receptors (TRPM8) respond to temperatures below body temperature.

questions.q: Hearing loss affects what proportion of the world population?
options: 5.3% of all people and one third of persons over 65
questions.explain: Disabling hearing loss affects 5.3% of the world population and one third of persons over 65 years. It is very prevalent globally. The molecular identity of the mechanotransduction channel in auditory hair cells remains incompletely established.

questions.q: The generator potential in a neural sensory receptor differs from an action potential in that:
options: Generator potentials are all-or-none like APs
Generator potentials vary in amplitude with stimulus strength and duration, unlike the fixed AP
Generator potentials propagate along axons without decrement
Generator potentials are always faster than APs
Generator potentials are triggered by K+ influx
questions.explain: Generator potentials (and receptor potentials) are GRADED — their amplitude varies continuously with stimulus strength and duration. This is the key difference from APs which are all-or-none. The graded generator potential is then converted to AP frequency at the spike initiation zone.

questions.q: Touch receptors respond to:
options: Many forms of physical contact: stretch, steady pressure, fluttering, vibration, and texture
questions.explain: Touch receptors are highly diverse — different receptor types respond to different mechanical stimuli: stretch, steady pressure, fluttering, vibration, and texture. They come as free nerve endings and as complex encapsulated receptors (Meissner's, Pacinian, Merkel's, etc.).

questions.q: Which statement about somatosensory cortex plasticity is TRUE?
options: The cortical map is fixed after birth
The cortical map can change according to the usage of the body part (plasticity)
Only the motor cortex shows plasticity
Plasticity requires damage to the brain
The cortical map is identical in all individuals
questions.explain: The somatosensory cortex map can change (plasticity) based on usage. For example, musicians who practice extensively develop larger cortical representations for their fingers. This is the neurological basis for sensory skill development and recovery after injury.

questions.q: The cochlea's mechanotransduction machinery in hair cells involves:
options: Voltage-gated Na+ channels like in neurons
Tip links connecting stereocilia that gate mechanotransduction channels when stereocilia deflect
Piezo2 channels identical to those in skin
questions.explain: In cochlear hair cells, deflection of the stereocilia bundle stretches tip links (extracellular filaments connecting adjacent stereocilia). This tension opens mechanotransduction ion channels at the tip of the shorter stereocilia, allowing K+ and Ca2+ to flow in and depolarize the hair cell.

questions.q: Why is the molecular identity of the mechanotransduction channel in auditory hair cells considered the 'last frontier of sensory neurobiology'?
options: Because hearing is the most complex sense
Because it remains incompletely identified despite all other major sensory channels having been cloned
Because hair cells are difficult to study
Because it requires specialized Nobel Prize-level technology only
questions.explain: Unlike most other sensory channels (TRPVs, TRPMs, Piezos) which have been cloned and characterized, the primary mechanotransduction channel complex in cochlear hair cells was only recently partially identified (TMC1/2 proteins) and is considered the last major unsolved problem in sensory channel biology.
